author: amy-why-3459
body: Is there a cleanup process after the benchmark ends?

author: amy-why-3459
body: Clean up debug logs?

author: gcanlin
body: Do you mean cleaning up the saved audio? I wanna add a save feature so that we can hear the real results by ourselves instead of only WER score.

author: gcanlin
body: Yes. I will push a commit to clean all of them.

author: gcanlin
body: Done.

author: linyueqian
body:
[High] Stream slots can leak on client abort. `_finish_stream_request` is the only place a slot is returned to the pool, and it is only reached when a payload arrives with `stream_finished`/`finished=True` from stage 0. If a client disconnects mid-stream (or the request is aborted engine-side), the slot stays in `_stream_req_slots` forever, along with its buffered `_stream_pending_codes`. With `codec_stream_slots: 8`, eight mid-stream disconnects push every later request onto the `decode_offline` starved path until the server restarts.

Could we release slots from the runner's finished/aborted req-ids path, or add a reclaim (e.g. timeout/LRU on requests that stop producing payloads)?

author: linyueqian
body:
[Medium] When a stream already classified as cumulative later fails the prefix match, this branch flips to delta mode but `chunk_np` is not re-sliced, so the entire accumulated waveform is re-emitted to the client as one chunk (audible rewind/duplicate). Commit a39a5617 had a `cumulative_snapshot_broken` warning on exactly this branch; the log cleanup removed the warning but kept the behavior, so the failure is now silent.

Suggest at least restoring the warning, and trimming to `prev_len` when the shapes still allow it. More generally, since the cumulative-vs-delta detection is content-based (`np.allclose` on the prefix with atol=1e-6), an explicit producer-side flag on the chunk metadata would make this deterministic instead of a runtime guess.

author: linyueqian
body:
[Medium, latent] The comment above says ceil division, but this floors, and `input_lengths` is not updated after the `F.pad` to a multiple of `downsample_rate`. For `batch_encode()` with unequal-length waveforms, the shorter samples' `audio_codes_lengths` get truncated by up to one frame (~80 ms of codes). Serving only exercises the decode path today so nothing hits this yet, but it is reachable through the public encode API.

`output_lengths = (input_lengths + self.patch_size - 1) // self.patch_size` would match the comment and the padding.

author: gcanlin
body: Make sense to me. Fixed.

author: gcanlin

author: hsliuustc0106
body: [High] This tries the v2 tokenizer for every `codec_path`, but the legacy `OpenMOSS-Team/MOSS-Audio-Tokenizer` config uses the same `model_type` and the v2 config accepts unknown fields/defaults. That means legacy MOSS checkpoints can instantiate as the v2/stereo/48k codec instead of falling back to `MossAudioTokenizerModel`, which can fail strict weight loading or decode with the wrong layout. Could we choose v2 only from a reliable marker, e.g. the v2 checkpoint/config fields such as `number_channels`/48k, and keep legacy checkpoints on the legacy class?

author: hsliuustc0106
body: [Medium] This narrows the previous parsing and now assumes `max_new_frames` is always a non-empty list. The old code accepted scalar values, empty/missing values, and invalid values gracefully; a caller passing `{"max_new_frames": 128}` will now crash with `int is not subscriptable` during preprocess. Serving currently passes a list, but preserving the old normalization here avoids a small compatibility regression.

author: gcanlin
body: But we can move it into modeling first.

author: hsliuustc0106
body:
This is now set unconditionally for every TTS model type (previously gated on `self._tts_model_type == "qwen3_tts"`). I traced it: the `tts_local_seed` key is only consumed by `gpu_model_runner._explicit_talker_seed` (MOSS-TTS-Local + qwen3-tts talker paths), and `qwen3_omni`/`fish_speech` silently ignore it, so there's no functional regression today.

Two small notes:
1. The key name `tts_local_seed` reads as MOSS-Local-specific but is now written for all models. If a future model adds a consumer reading this key, it would silently pick up deterministic seeding for any client `seed`. Worth either a more generic name (`talker_seed`) or a comment noting it's intentionally shared.
2. The deepcopy is now unconditional (previously only when `sampling_params_list is self.engine_client.default_sampling_params_list`). Negligible at TTS request rates, just confirming it's intended.

author: hsliuustc0106
body:
This cleanup hook only covers `finished_req_ids`, but aborted requests can also be removed via the `unscheduled_req_ids` path below (around line 89) without ever appearing in `finished_req_ids`. If a client disconnects mid-stream and the request is reaped through that path, `on_requests_finished` won't fire for it, so `_MossCodecStreamSession` would keep its acquired slot and buffered codes.

The session guards against total exhaustion with a `logger.warning` + buffer-until-free fallback, so it degrades rather than crashes. But a sustained abort pattern could starve the stream-slot pool. Could you confirm whether aborted req_ids are guaranteed to flow through `finished_req_ids` on the generation-runner path here, or whether the unscheduled removal path should also call `on_requests_finished`?

author: hsliuustc0106
body:
`_MossCodecStreamSession` holds a persistent `codec.streaming(batch_size)` ExitStack acquired here. `release()` resets individual slots (good), but `_MossCodecStreamSession.close()` is never called from `MossTTSCodecDecoder` — there's no `__del__`, `atexit`, or engine-shutdown hook wired to it.

In normal serving this is process-lifetime state and harmless. But `StreamingModule.streaming()` registers a `_stop_streaming` callback on the ExitStack; if `close()` is never invoked that teardown never runs. If the codec module is ever re-created in-process (tests, multi-engine, reload) the previous ExitStack callback is orphaned. A `__del__`/engine-shutdown hook calling `self._stream_session.close()` would make the lifecycle symmetric. Low priority — flagging since the session is the one persistent stateful resource added here.

author: hsliuustc0106
body:
This is now set unconditionally for every TTS model type (previously gated on `self._tts_model_type == "qwen3_tts"`). I traced it: the `tts_local_seed` key is only consumed by `gpu_model_runner._explicit_talker_seed` (MOSS-TTS-Local + qwen3-tts talker paths), and `qwen3_omni`/`fish_speech` silently ignore it, so there's no functional regression today.

Two small notes:
1. The key name `tts_local_seed` reads as MOSS-Local-specific but is now written for all models. If a future model adds a consumer reading this key, it would silently pick up deterministic seeding for any client `seed`. Worth either a more generic name (`talker_seed`) or a comment noting it's intentionally shared.
2. The deepcopy is now unconditional (previously only when `sampling_params_list is self.engine_client.default_sampling_params_list`). Negligible at TTS request rates, just confirming it's intended.

The session guards against total exhaustion with a `logger.warning` + buffer-until-free fallback, so it degrades rather than crashes. But a sustained abort pattern could starve the stream-slot pool. Could you confirm whether aborted req_ids are guaranteed to flow through `finished_req_ids` on the generation-runner path here, or whether the unscheduled removal path should also call `on_requests_finished`?

author: hsliuustc0106
body:
`_MossCodecStreamSession` holds a persistent `codec.streaming(batch_size)` ExitStack acquired here. `release()` resets individual slots (good), but `_MossCodecStreamSession.close()` is never called from `MossTTSCodecDecoder` — there's no `__del__`, `atexit`, or engine-shutdown hook wired to it.

In normal serving this is process-lifetime state and harmless. But `StreamingModule.streaming()` registers a `_stop_streaming` callback on the ExitStack; if `close()` is never invoked that teardown never runs. If the codec module is ever re-created in-process (tests, multi-engine, reload) the previous ExitStack callback is orphaned. A `__del__`/engine-shutdown hook calling `self._stream_session.close()` would make the lifecycle symmetric. Low priority — flagging since the session is the one persistent stateful resource added here.
